sentences.text: The trophy would not fit in the suitcase because it was too big.
sentences.why: Winograd-style pronoun coreference: to make sense of “it,” something inside the model has to look back at “trophy.”
sentences.note: Expected a coreference lookup from “it” to “trophy”, but the best head only puts 28% there.

sentences.text: She did not eat anything at the party last night.
sentences.why: Negation scope: “anything” is only grammatical because “not” came earlier — a head has to track the negation that licenses it.
sentences.note: From “anything,” 41% of this head’s attention goes straight to “not” — the word that licenses it.

sentences.text: The keys to the old wooden cabinet were on the table.
sentences.why: Long-range subject–verb agreement: “were” agrees with “keys,” not the nearer singular “cabinet.” The head must skip the distractor.
sentences.note: “were” reaches over “cabinet” back to its real subject “keys” with 82% of this head’s attention.

sentences.text: He decided to spill the beans about the surprise party.
sentences.why: Idiom binding: “beans” only means secrets next to “spill” — the pair behaves like one unit, and a head links the two halves.
sentences.note: “beans” binds back to “spill” with 86% of this head’s attention — the idiom is stored as a pair.

sentences.text: When John and Mary went to the store, John handed a drink to Mary.
sentences.why: Repeated-name binding: the second “John” looks back at the first — duplicate-token heads like this one are an ingredient of in-context learning.
sentences.note: The second “John” finds the first with 63% of this head’s attention — a duplicate-token head at work.

sentences.text: The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.
sentences.why: A previous-token head: some heads ignore meaning entirely and just attend one position back — pure positional plumbing that later layers build on.
sentences.note: From “fox,” 100% of this head’s attention sits on the previous token “brown” — and it does the same from every position. Pure plumbing.

sentences.text: The book that the professor whom the students admired wrote became a bestseller.
sentences.why: Break it: “became” needs its subject “book,” buried behind two stacked relative clauses. Watch what the attention does instead.
sentences.note: Surprise: even across the clause pile-up, 33% of this head’s attention still finds “book.”

sentences.text: The horse raced past the barn fell.
sentences.why: Break it: the classic garden-path sentence. “fell” is the real verb of “horse” — but the model has already committed to the wrong parse of “raced.”
sentences.note: Surprise: 81% of this head’s attention recovers the “horse–fell” link despite the garden path.
